It's in-depth but could spur some thinking now that informs how he views the business/marketing strategy.

I spoke with Ms. Carolyn Smith and her son about their business idea. They want to start a owner operator trucking company. Currently, Ms. Smith two sons have been driving as a long haul transport driver for many years and they will be part of the business. Their mission is to deliver freight and long haul service for companies in 48 states. I explained to them that having experience as a driver is great however, understanding all of the parts of the business is essential to be successful. I suggested after conversing with them about their startup cost that my recommendation is that they attend our business plan class in order for them to understand all the things that is needed to start a successful business. I shared with them that the business plan process will aid them in understanding their initial expenses, including vehicle acquisition, insurance, permits, and licensing fees. I asked them about how they would price their service and what other cost they might have to pay. I s

I spoke with Joyce, Roger's wife. They have opened a pizza shop in Gladwin. Joyce had not intended on being involved but feels the need to help where she can. Early on the restaurant has been well received. Joyce has many questions about whether they can reach breakeven early on while being able to pay Roger for all the work he is putting in to get it started. If they had additional equipment they could do more in house and safe some money. We got through the Intake Session.


We will need to meet again. Joyce has many more questions. We will need to take a look at the business plan together to see if it makes sense. She said she will set up a meeting for early next week to tackle her other questions.

Continued entering information in the Excel Financial Forecasting tool. We added current staff and staff when the new 12 bed AFC will be open. We entered the operating expenses and sales forecast. When we completed the entry of information, we reviewed the cash flow and Income Statement. I shared how the tool works and that the first five tabs will populate into tabs 6 and beyond, as the tool is formula driven. The outcome was a significant loss for 2025 and 2026. I recommended they look at their records and provide me with actual numbers for ytd in sales, operating expenses, cogs and payroll.


Plan: We will meet again next Friday, June 20th at 10am.

Check on Haley's bookkeeping and business health


Reviewed Haley's 2025 spreadsheet

She has a few items to follow up on and will email me once complete; otherwise, I will text her in one week.

She forgets to enter personal expenses, such as groceries, that we are tracking to encourage fiscal responsibility.


Now that we have business data, we can review performance

Hailey's income is stable, and she can not grow her income without raising prices (schedule is full) or having her 2nd nail tech work more hours

Advised that she should consider a price increas,e as it has been 8 months since she did so

Looked at past COGS 3.67 in 2023, 5.26 in 202,4 and 7.43 in 2025 (so far)


Pricing will be a topic at our next meeting. Haley should think about pricing before then.

Suggested at least 5-10% and showed her the impact on her bottom line.


Haley will schedule our next meeting

Hi Wendy,


Someone from Michigan SBDC recommended I reach out to you with a quick question.


I had an LLC in Michigan in 2011 doing consulting for business management and design. Then I shifted to a full time job for a number of years.


Now I'm seeking to re-start my same consulting business. As I understand it my federal EIN doesn't expire but I need to reinstate my business in the state of Michigan. 


My question to you is, do I use form CD-710 for restated articles of organization (for use by domestic limited liability companies)? It's the same articles as before, I'm just looking to restart it with same purpose and intent as before.

Operating at a breakeven level. Boating industry is still very weak. The company has about $250,000 left in cash. It things do not pick up he may lay everyone off for a month. Still fighting with MiOSHA over the need for paint booths. Scheduled for a trial in front to a magistrate in November.

Now that Jerry sold RTI Labs, he continues with the Peerspectives Group an owner of Khemia Software. He uses 4 KPI's for the companies he ownsL (1) Revenue per FTE, (2) payroll as a % of sales (42% for RTI Labs), (3) material a a % of sale (12% for RTI Labs) and A/R days sales (60 days). Jerry is going through SCORE training currently.

Hi Kevin,


I got my first consult for combustible dust hazard analysis (DHA). This was required by an insurance company for my client but it is an OSHA requirement too. 


Some dusts are combustible and if a powder of it disperses in the air it can explode. I am qualified to do this assessment.  


My client got a quote from an engineering company for $34000 and I did it for $5000 plus cost which came out to 1/3 of the 34K they were quoted. The insurance company was pleased with my report so I'm happy , but I haven't worked since.


I'm trying to market via Link-in and Alienable, but no bites otherwise.

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Hi Jimmie,

Thanks again for your patience as I work through a busy client schedule. It was great meeting with you last week and hearing more about your restaurant idea.

Quick recap:

You’re currently looking into the cost of a commercial kitchen hood so you can include it in your business plan. You're also actively working on building your credit, which is currently at 650—a solid starting point for future financing options.

Next steps & suggestions:

  • I recommend gathering 2–3 quotes for kitchen hoods from restaurant equipment suppliers (new or used). This will give your plan more accuracy and help with financing conversations.
  • Keep an eye on your credit activity—paying down any existing balances and avoiding new debt will help raise your score further.
  • Once you have that hood pricing, I can help plug it into your business plan and go over funding options together.

Client shared proposed lease and MOU for a home in Lake City intended to serve as a residential adult foster care business. Consultant provided a strategic analysis of the lease terms and advised on negotiation points, with the following key observations and recommendations:

1. Confidentiality:

Client asked that advice remain confidential and not be shared directly with the property manager. Consultant scheduled follow-up discussion before client responds.

2. Overall Framing:

  • Emphasized that negotiation is normal and not adversarial; encouraged client to view landlord as a potential partner.
  • Strong recommendation for client to retain legal counsel due to the significance of this lease for the viability of the business.

3. Rent Concerns:

  • $5,000/month appears above-market for Lake City. Consultant noted comparable homes typically rent for $2,500–$3,000.
  • Lease bundles
She sent me a typed version of her plan. We need to review a cash flow

Summarize this email

I spoke with our client today by telephone. He used a registered agent service to set up his company, Oak and Iron Hospitality LLC earlier in June 2025. We discussed his plans to create an upscale dining experience for the mid Michigan area. He has a business plan that will need to be updated. I gave him access to LivePlan in which to update his plan.


His greatest need is to find a suitable location. I suggested he speak with Kati Mora at MMDC. He would also need a liquor license for his restaurant. He is wanting to bring in an equity partner as he only has $30k at this point to invest. He will need to determine what renovations will be needed specific to the property he chooses. That will drive what costs he must cover and how much equity he will need to raise. The remainder he would cover with an SBA loan. Email sent:

Along with Emily Gibson, met with Julia and her CPA, Justin Cook, at RFM Group offices in Mt Pleasant.

  • Purpose of the meeting with to inform Justin of Julia's plan to sell the business. Wanted him to know this and how the process of selling would be selling the idea of what Valhalla could be.
  • Communicated to him the importance of this before he completed the businesses 2024 tax return. Acknowledged there is little he can do in adjusting her Schedule C, especially in revenue and COGS, but suggested her expenses be considered when doing tax return.
  • Also introduced Justin to the roll of SBDC.

Myself and Business Growth Consultant Jeff Punches, met with Julia at Robert F. Murray in Mt. Pleasant, along with her accountant, Justin. Julia disclosed to Justin that she is planning to sell the business. We discussed her 2024 tax return for the business, which Justin has not filed yet. We discussed the ways in which we can help explain expenses to potential buyers, as well as ways to accurately show the business' profitability in the tax filing.

Once the 2024 return is complete, Jeff and I plan to use LivePlan to build a potential revenue model and narrative to present to potential buyers for consideration. Justin will be starting on the returns shortly, and we should be able to move forward by late June/early July.

Kayo and I met with Ryan at the Innovate Marquette SmartZone. We discussed their current federal funding and outlook for future funds. They discussed the progress of their technology and research. We shared two funding/program opportunities, VentureWell and Gener8tor.

Hi Aaron,

Thanks again for your patience—I'm currently working with a high number of clients, but I wanted to follow up on our meeting from earlier this week.

Here's a quick summary of what we covered:

You're in the final stages of preparing your mobile cryotherapy lounge concept, with strong connections already forming to help extend your operations year-round (like with WMU athletics). You’ve got a contractor for your trailer and a meeting with a business attorney coming up—great steps. I was genuinely impressed with how much you’ve already accomplished.

As requested, I’ve attached the Client Release Form you’ll need to sign if you’d like me to share my feedback with the Kalamazoo Forward Ventures team.

When you have a chance, please send me your financial projections. Once I receive them, I’ll do a final review and also loop in our Senior Business Consultant, Wendy, for one more round of

Kayo and I met with Wael Khouli at the Innovate Marquette SmartZone. Wael discussed their technology including the transport of health data and being bound by HIPAA. They are training AI on mock data. There is a challenge around actual medical data scans due to the poor image quality. They are actively trying to work with Henry Ford. Their pricing model is not yet set, and they are considering a hybrid model with a pay per use plus commission on success. They are interested in connected with U of M Sparrow. They are or have received funding from BRCC. AuthSnap is working with Mayo Clinic. Kayo suggested focusing on pre-authorizations for generating success data for investors and customers. They are looking to raise $500,000 to prove out their model. An investor has soft circled some funds. I recommended they look at smaller channel partners. Wael mentioned they need about 400 cases in order to prove the model.

Made a first draft of the Executive Summary from our prior discussion and we discussed the missing pieces in order to complete it. We discussed the possibility of incorporating a loyalty plan into a white label hosting service for his client's landing pages. It would be beneficial to get ARR in addition to his per order business, and this can be accomplished through offering hosting and 'loyalty as a service, LAAS' (tm). I explained my previous experience with a loyalty program for Universal Vivendi/Sierra Games online, and how it could apply to his business, locking in customers and introducing friction for moving to competing services. We discussed the potential of expanding the site's purview to include other marketing services. I explained the advantages of test marketing using Google analytics, and how those metrics would play into any external funding. He has signed his sales associate to a sales agreement, as strongly advised, and will send a copy of the agree

Working with Dana from Sprout - asked me a lot of licensing/health dept questions. I suggested she reach back out to Dana. I can help for certain with business plan and financials. May be best to wait 'til end of season when she has actual income/expenses so that we can work on the projections into 2026, 2027 to enable her to take this full-time. I'm reaching out to Dana to suggest she and Timeya talk and then I'll reach back out to Timeya in about a month.

Key takeaways

- Traditional bank loans are not suitable for new product ventures due to lack of history and proof of concept

- Crowdfunding platforms discussed: Kickstarter, Indiegogo, Kiva Detroit, and Patronicity

- Intellectual property protection needs consideration before public disclosure

- Insurance should be activated when product is ready for public distribution

- Scam alerts regarding business filings and certifications discussed

Met with David L. for a consultation about his manufacturing business, which will focus on building and selling ~750-gallon live haul fish tanks for transporting fish. He has experience in aquaculture and environmental health. He reported that everything is going well and he was heading to the bank that day to get business matters in order.

David has his LLC set up and owns most of the equipment he needs. He is not currently seeking financing. His primary questions included:

  • How should he be taxed?
  • How should he pay himself?
  • Should he remain an LLC?
  • How/when to write off expenses?

He also mentioned interest in government procurement opportunities and wants to ensure full compliance. He’s seeking SBDC guidance on structure, compliance, and tax strategy.

Dr Martin is retired. His wife collected dolls and there is a significant quantity in their collection. He has been paring down the collection by giving them away to various charities, but one of his grandchildren suggested they be sold and the money used for college expenses.

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes: Lance Wolfe <lwolfe@mtu.edu>

Teresa is navigating the complex challenges of commercializing multiple advanced navigation technologies while balancing her role as a physics professor and CEO. Her team has promising IP around radio-frequency celestial navigation, magnetic anomaly-based systems, and neutron detection for rare earth and landmine detection. However, the company is at a critical inflection point: they need to choose a focused beachhead market, secure early traction, and craft a compelling story that resonates with potential funders. Teresa is also facing the reality of long military acquisition timelines and limited experience with SBIR grant processes, all while maintaining clarity about which technologies to prioritize and how to structure the messaging around each.


I recommended that Teresa narrow her pitch to one core technology, tailored for a specific market segment like mineral detection or private aviation, and build her storyline around the end-user problem instead of the te

Christie is navigating multiple layers of complexity as she develops Choreo, her AI-enabled oncology navigation platform, while also considering launching a separate, traditional nurse navigation agency. She’s balancing grant applications, pitch development, and financial modeling—all while conducting customer discovery and vetting technology partners. Some of the key challenges include uncertainty about Choreo’s interoperability claims, aligning pricing models with true service costs, refining projections to reflect realistic development and sales timelines, and structuring the business for long-term scalability. Christie is also working through concerns about startup overhead—such as high registered agent fees—and determining the best way to grow her offering while preserving credibility and financial sustainability.


I advised Christie to unbundle the AI navigation service and nurse staffing into separate business models, with the option of launching the tradition

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Summary: The client sent their rejection from DoT. She is bummed that they did not get the SBIR. She also sent an email asking if she should change her address in her SAM.gov registration to reflect her MI company registration.


Action: I responded that I am so sorry to hear of this. I can’t see the results – did it include a debrief? I would be interested to read that if it did to understand the reasoning. For their question from yesterday, now that this award wasn’t made, I would edit SAM.gov and correct the information therein. SAM.gov has to be renewed every year to keep it active. If it falls inactive, the process or verification has to start over again, which is why I recommend making sure to do it before their anniversary date.


Plan: No plan at this time.

Summary: The client sent the two funding opportunities he’s targeting: DoD SBIR 25.3: he’d like to review topics related to AI, autonomy, and lawful decision-making as they become available. His technology is a strong fit for defense-focused challenges involving real-time AI alignment and auditability. AI Safety Fund: https://www.safe.ai/fund

This philanthropic grant supports early-stage AI safety research. he believes CODE his ethical oversight layer is directly aligned with their mission. At this stage, he’d love to begin working from their template for Phase-I DoD proposals. He’s especially interested in getting Volume 1 right while leaving flexibility to tailor the content to the topic once it’s released. He’ll be finalizing the demo, refining supporting materials, and beginning proposal drafting all in parallel.  


Action: I responded for the DoD SBIR 25.3, it looks like it is expected to open for pre-solicitation on 8/6. Their colleagues at BBC, Etc. are

If that is the case, and if they are looking for a review from me, I would recommend that they simply send me the document and I will review and get it back to them ASAP. I am leaving on vacation next Friday (6/20) and will not be back until after the proposals are due, hence my desire to simplify the process for them. If they are not doing NSF or if they have questions for me beyond the need for a review, I am happy to

Austin is making good progress, saying that she is ahead of her sales expectations. She is getting some good word of mouth and feels that her FB posts are helping. She knows she's gotten some new business from clients who's farriers raised their prices. She's excited about the videos that were created through LRL, and she mentioned that the Marketing presentation at LRL was very AI-focused and she's not interested in exploring that at this time.


We discussed financial forecasting and seasonality. Austin has obtained her LLC and she had a few questions about that. She also had some tax questions that we discussed, although I encouraged her to talk with her CPA and her farrier network. We also discussed inflation, tariffs and the impact those could have on her clients and the industry as a whole.

Kayo and I met with David of GrowthFinder. His startup is focused on CliftonStrengths and uses AI to generate a personalized professional development plan. The tool uses the CliftonStrengths profile, job description, and other data to create the plan. We suggested they target professional employment organizations (PEO) such as AxiosHR, Michigan Works, and other talent firms. We helped David identify a new value proposition: reducing employee churn, improving culture and productivity, and saving costs.

Deanna reached out by phone to let me know that they were not selected for the GLBBH grant awards. She has not received the rubric grading yet, but we decided that we should talk about it once she is able to review those. Elizabeth of CMURC will likely be sending them out shortly.

Client really needs a store manager based on not being able to be at the store all the time. Currently has 2 part-time staff. I asked if I could reach out to a connection and received her verbal permission to create the introduction. (documenting in an email follow up) Needs to get a handle on financials. Not her forte. We talked through COGS and why understanding that allows her to focus on inventory of her most lucrative products. She can still have some less lucrative items if that is what draws people from the window display. In the end, she wants to focus more on corporate gifts as well since that instinctively is her most profitable item. Client booked another session to review financials on July 11th.

Want to expand the detailing business into tires. Not sure whether to expand in the current location, need to check with the city as they have a zoning waiver to run the business out of their home that expires, or find a new location.

Looking for financing, but not sure how much they will need.

Hi Yuki,


It was great meeting with you and learning more about your vision for launching a Japanese-style fast food restaurant. I’m excited to support you on this journey and wanted to send a quick recap of what we discussed:

You’re currently employed through July 31st and looking to transition into entrepreneurship after over 20 years in the corporate world. Your business concept is inspired by Panda Express, with a focus on delivery and pickup orders. While you may include a small dine-in area, your main goal is to serve food through platforms like Uber Eats and DoorDash, especially since you don’t have a delivery vehicle of your own right now.

You’ve decided not to offer sushi due to sanitary considerations and are still in the early planning phase, so a pricing strategy for your menu is still in development.

You’ll be living in Three Oaks, Michigan, and are seeking a business location no more than 30 minutes away, ideally in an area with high p

Has worked on her 36-month plan. Is working with Tami @ Honor CU (Tami referred client to us). Client learned that for every $1 in expenses, she should have $1.20 in sales to cover expenses and cash flow. We discussed more about cash flow vs profit. We walked through the EIN today and Dani did it directly herself. (did the LLC on her own last week). Working on her domain still and then the website/FB page. Client isn't sure what's next except for financials. I agreed and we can keep refining. Client's mom may provide cash investment or may buy the property and lease it back to my client. Still working on it. Next session is booked for 6/23

Pricing

I met with Andy to discuss pricing. Andy provided me with a breakdown of the cost of inputs to create his BBQ Rub, his BBQ Sauce and what consumables go into a custom pork butt. Through our discussions I was able to learn that it costs andy. approx. $0.20/lb of meat for his spice rub a total of 13.09 for a batch of rub. For each event Andy makes his custom BBQ sauce. For each event he provides the customer with at minimum a gallon of sauce. The gallon of sauce costs 22.16 to produce.


For his custom pork shoulders we determined a system for him to calculate his costs. Excluding Labor, per shoulder it costs 89.90 for a shoulder. The size of the shoulder affects the amount of rub applied but it holds his consumables (including fuel) constant


We used a 10lb pork shoulder as a standard for ease of calculation. For a standard 10lb shoulder he should charge $259.99


I will be following up with Andy with the u

Kayo and I met with Jennifer of Quickie Tech. She is creating an app for couples intimacy. Jennifer background in couples/sex therapy, and works at NMU. Her technology utilizes evidence based background. She has a clickable wireframe. Couples are the end user, but not necessarily the customer. Jennifer is considering a subscription model and advertising. Her technology does not record client data as it is bound by HIPAA.

Jurnee texted me to let me know she was not awarded a GLBBH grant. We plan to meet on the 24th to discuss the grant rubric grading, as well as discuss other opportunities that might be helpful. Jurnee is particularly interested in potential pitch competitions.

Joe and Chelsea

No Facebook - The client no longer want to use Facebook to market her business, it doesn't a line with her values of getting out from behind the computer and getting into nature.


Tammy said they covered in her classes that email newsletters can be successful so we talked about how to grow an email list of followers. Tammy will be attending farmers markets and local festivals. We talked about a drawing to encourage email submission. She can give away her time on a service she plans to offer.


We laid out a brief timeline outlining the writing of the blog that will be highlighted in the eNewsletter and featured on her webpage. We set a goal of July 8th to send out the eNewletter to the people she collects at the 4th of July craft show.

good session - did EIN and revised the 36-month projections. Suggested Dani run the revised numbers past lender (Tami Pitale with Honor CU) and see what she has to say.

AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

1:1 consultation and updates on business progress. In-person meetup. We discussed updates on his business progress and addressed several concerns he raised. David inquired about additional funding opportunities after participating in the Verizon Digital Business Grant Competition, but with the grant season temporarily wrapped up, we struggled to identify new options for him to pursue in the immediate future. 


He also presented some paperwork he received in the mail, which we reviewed and determined to be junk mail from companies attempting to exploit his new CAGE number and registration with Sam.gov. Additionally, David expressed confusion about his status as a registered Michigan vendor, having attempted to use the self-serve Michigan vendor portal to obtain a SIGMA number for bidding on state-level contracts, but he forgot his username after setting up an account w

Summary: The client wrote that he understands that it’s too early to determine what the topics will be for DoD 25.3, but he was wondering if it might be possible to see an example of a generic proposal. He thinks having that context would help put his mind at ease as he prepares, and help him understand how his ideas will eventually need to fit into the structure.


Action: I responded that all of the departments within the DoD have different format requirements for the SBIRs and even the formats for the same department can change quite drastically from solicitation to solicitation. I have attached the latest that I have for Air Force, Navy ,and Army (in this particular cycle, Army had both a white paper and a slide deck required – that is not always the case) for they to peruse. But it is VERY, VERY important that they don’t actually use one of these as a guide because wherever they find a topic, the format will most likely be very different from any of these.

<

Summary: The client wrote that, unfortunately, they received news that they were not selected for award. The main reason cited was that there is “marginal potential for commercialization” (not mentioned, but this may be because they proposed using a research-grade dash cam ( $35k) for Phase 1, and they may not have clearly explained that they intended to switch to an off-the-shelf consumer (~$150) device for the commercialization phase). They attached their evaluation report. 3 judges scored them. Light color indicates the vote of 1 judge and dark color indicates the vote of 2 or more judges. They scored highest in the categories “Technical Personnel” and “Understanding of the Problem”.


Action: I responded that I am sorry to hear, but those are pretty good reviews for a first try, commercialization potential excluded. They know where to focus. Have they considered another agency to apply to? DoT will not open again until next year, but this seems like interesting t

Summary: The client responded that they are planning to submit an NSF FAST-TRACK SBIR proposal by the July 2 deadline and would greatly appreciate their feedback. He sent the broader impacts and commercialization plan for review and will be adding the summary and Phase I project description by tomorrow evening after one more round of edits. The initial draft of the Phase II R&D plan is in progress and will be uploaded by Tuesday.


Action: I responded that I got all of their proposal pieces and will take a look at them. I also asked Joe, if he could take a look at the commercialization plan for content. Because this is a FastTrack, the commercial planning aspect is supposed to be further along than a Phase I general overview. Having insight on red flags, concerns and/or general suggestions from a non-SBIR perspective would be helpful. I have reprinted the general instructions below so they can see what they are looking for. “Commercialization Plan – 5 pages MAXIM

Provided client with final comments on business plan sections


TRose Malone <trose.businesscoach@gmail.com></

AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM: He needs to refinance his loans and investment

 

RECOMMENDATIONS AND ACTIONS TO BE TAKEN: I talked with Matt from Frnakenmuth CU about this case. I sent a consent form to Scott too. I began to look over his previous cash flow to see how to amend it for a refi. We reviewed the amount needed...about $545,000 for an LC and investments made by Scoot and inventory. Matt discusses how the financing would work....debenture, then split the funding into Franenmuth 40%, MCDC, 50%, and Scott 10%. I talked with Scott too about this, and he thought he would need $1,000,000...a difference. I got some rates from Matt too...20 years for FCU and maybe more for MCDC.

Checked out the food truck at Bridgefest and met and spoke briefly with Pam's husband. According to him, business is steady. They are busy with the food truck business. The food truck is quite large and well-marked with the business name clearly visible.

Franco is working to bring his fintech startup Haikpi to market, offering a KPI-tracking and financial analytics platform for mid-sized businesses through accounting system integration. His main challenges include finalizing the last 10% of software development, establishing business infrastructure such as the LLC and Stripe integration, and navigating the legal complexities of co-founder roles and international student status. Franco also faces a common startup dilemma: the temptation to push for early sales before completing a robust customer discovery process that could clarify which segment—such as engineering firms or small manufacturers—should be his beachhead market. His conversations so far have highlighted pain points like high analyst costs, poor financial metric visibility, and limited strategic insights for leadership teams.


I advised Franco to prioritize building a strong foundation through customer discovery instead of rushing into sales. We discussed

Summary: The client sent me their NSF Phase I proposal for review.


Action: I have taken a look at their document, and it is very well written as always. That said, I do have several comments, suggestions, and recommendations for they. If they remember, the way I review is to start with general comments about the proposal and then take each section, one by one, in order. Hopefully, this makes it easier for they to follow along with their document. Again, they know their technology best. While I will review as I would if I were their reviewer(s), if something I say or suggest goes against what they think is right, they should certainly ignore it. While I am good at what I do, no one is perfect, and I might not understand what they are trying to convey. Of course, if that happens, then that is an indication that they haven’t been as clear as possible in the way they wrote, and they might want to look that section over again.  Starting with general comments, as I am su

Hi Alain,


I was referred by Joseph Carr and Arcadio Ramirez.

On Fri, Jun 13, 2025 at 11:21 AM Alain Piette <piettea@gvsu.edu> wrote:


Dear David, thank you for registering the mission small business development center. My team and I look forward to supporting you. You mentioned that you were referred to by an SBDC colleague. Can you please let me know who that person is so that I can update my salesforce record.

Summary: They client asked if I could help him identify any forms or procedures that need to be completed so their proposal won’t be rejected from some formality error.


Action: I responded that SAM.gov is all they need to do ahead of time. Make sure that they do the FULL registration for both contracts and grants which will be required for DoD. The government can sometimes take weeks (or months, but that hasn’t happened since Covid days) to process it, so it is important to do well ahead of time. In the shorter term, the other registrations they will need are the SBIR registry (https://app.www.sbir.gov/company-registration/overview) and the DoD submission site (https://www.dodsbirsttr.mil/submissions/login). Both of these are instantaneous registration, but if they want to do them ahead of time, it is great to get them out of the way.


Plan: We set up a meeting for Tuesday July 8th at 11am. That will give me enough time to look over what they have

Media.Other: SBDC Client - I have known from being advisor to other startups

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Summary: The client asked if: “1. Do they know any companies/organization that provides grant writing services/help for startups that specializes in DOD? 2. Is there any funding available to startups to support such an engagement.” Janice Seabolt for AA Spark responded that BBCetc does a great job supporting grant applications for DoD. They offer a discounted rate through a grant from the State of Michigan that offers 10 hrs of support for $500.


Action: I responded that Janis had a great suggestion in the BBC, Etc. but it is important to note that they will not help they write the document. The operate much like I do in assisting with templates, reviews and submission assistance. Monique is great in the DoD space. They can submit a request for an initial meeting here. There are no specific contract writing firms for DoD that I am aware of in Michigan. There are national ones such as Eva Garland, but they are very pricey. No, there are no available specific funds to

Summary: Scott wrote introducing Teresa & Kael, founders of Navigationis. They've previous done an SBIR and more are likely in their future so an intro seemed appropriate.


Action: I responded it is nice to meet them through Scott. I have moved him to BCC to save his inbox but please feel free to reach out to him in the future for anything that they need to work on together. I will start by giving they a little of my background so that they can see if there is something that I can assist they with. I am a high-energy physicist who had a small business in Virginia before coming to Michigan. The R&D of their particle accelerator research company was funded by the SBIR and STTR programs and they had over 30 awards (phase I and II) in 8 of the federal agencies before selling off the business in the early 2000s. Instead of forming another business, I came to the SBDC to help others utilize the SBIR/STTR and other federal grant and contract programs.   I work

Summary/Action: The client asked me for a review of their commercialization and broader impacts statements for review. Action: After a first pass, I wrote them and asked if they please tell me when they received their invitation to submit this document? I am very concerned about its content in lieu of the recent changes to the NSF priorities. Specifically, I am referencing this item: “Are they still funding research on misinformation/disinformation? Per the Presidential Action announced January 20, 2025, NSF will not prioritize research proposals that engage in or facilitate any conduct that would unconstitutionally abridge the free speech of any American citizen. NSF will not support research with the goal of combating "misinformation," "disinformation," and "malinformation" that could be used to infringe on the constitutionally protected speech rights of American citizens across the United States in a manner that advances a preferred narrative about s

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

The business is seeking to improve online visibility and drive more traffic to their website but is uncertain about the return on investment (ROI) of paid advertising via CVM. They are considering a limited 3-month test on one network to evaluate its effectiveness.

BRECOMMENDATIONS AND ACTIONS TO BE TAKEN:

  • Begin with a 3-month ad run on one CVM network to minimize risk and test efficacy.
  • Track website metrics before, during, and after the campaign, focusing on:
    • Website traffic volume
    • Referral sources (specifically CVM referrals)
    • Visitor engagement (time on site, page views, conversions)
  • Determine if this can be funded or supported through the Hub.
  • Set up clear tracking tools such as Google Analytics and UTM codes to measure campaign success.

Good Morning Jay,

I hope this message finds you well! I wanted to share some exciting news — I recently completed my massage therapy certification and passed the MBLEX. I’m now offering massage therapy as part of my business, alongside personal training, assisted table-based stretching, and therapeutic cupping.

I currently have a website through GoDaddy that a friend helped me build about two years ago. While it’s functional, I’d really like to sit down with someone in person to help me clean it up, make some updates, and learn how to manage it more confidently on my own moving forward. I’m happy to pay for the help, but I’d also love to learn in the process so I’m not always relying on others.

Do you happen to know of any local resources, classes, or individuals who work with entrepreneurs on web development or small business tools? I learn best face-to-face and would prefer that over communicating through email.

Any suggestions or feedback you have would be

Brady is a gigging musician and aslo teaches at MI Music. His wife is a 2nd grade teacher in Kalkaska. His parents are selling some properties and might give him $10k towards his $60k need. He has a finance degree and studied jazz at MSU (no degree) He got an audio engineering degree (?) and worked in LA for years. He moved back to NMC and was the program coordinator for the audio tech program until he was fired last fall. Now he wants to create a gym membership model of a place where people can use the space for audio work and get training with instruments and create community.

He's looking to lease space for $4000 in Milliken Ave in TC. He has a breakdown of money needed as follows:

  • 12k lease
  • 30k equipment
  • 10k soundproofing
  • 3k marketing
  • 3k permits and insurance.

Utilities included. Only needs to pay interet (he says) .


I talked to him about his ability to get a loan for a start-up and how to do it.

He stated that the LARA site sent him to Legal Zoom to fill out the articles of organization, and was confused. Attempted to connect with him via Zoom so I could see the computer screen, but he is not real techy so he couldn't figure it out.


RECOMMENDATIONS: We talked a bit, but decided its best to meet in person to show him how to use Zoom and to help him file his LLC articles of incorporation. Really didn't want him to get into the wrong place online. Set appointment in person for tomorrow in Bad Axe at 2pm.


NEXT STEPS:

  1. After filing the llc and making sure his domain registration is correct, he will be taking a break to winterize the cottage they moved into, but is interested in joining the cohort for fall.

Hi Quatisa,

Call with Karyn. We talked about the MOU and looked at the financials. $5000 in rent is too much. We discussed negotiating and how to do it. We went over many factors, but the rent is the biggest, followed by the $200 deductible on all repairs. We talked about the strategy. She is willing to walk away, which is a good way to negotiate. They are rushing her and must be under some financial pressure. It's an opportunity for her but she is taking all the risk and needs the profits. I re-worked a live Plan scenario and $5k is too high for rent. If she gets what she wants, her net profit is 12%. At $5k, its 7%. And it's tight. Her food and labor costs might be too low in the projection.


Karyn is going to send me her draft email that she plans to send to Stacey as the negotiations continue.

This session entry is from a meeting with a client that took place on April 30, 2025:

Christie is navigating the complex process of building out financial projections for her oncology navigation platform and preparing for both a grant application and investor pitch. She’s working with a new developer, Alpha Django, and expects development costs to range between $233,000 and $365,000 across phases. One of her main challenges is aligning financial projections with updated funding timelines, managing delayed employee start dates, and calculating benefits and payroll taxes accurately. Additionally, she's preparing for an NIH STTR grant submission and incorporating investor-ready financials into her updated pitch deck.


I introduced her to a new Google Sheets forecasting tool that simplifies modeling revenue, expenses, and hiring plans. I walked her through the setup and showed her how the tool links a cap table, balance sheet, and salary projections to generat

Grant commercialization application first round review. The application has good foundations, but is lacking in specifics, citations and comparisons to justify the resulting numbers for market size, pricing model, and the financial forecast had some fundamental errors such as S&M being included in COGS, and no lag between sales and revenue recognition. Repeating round numbers indicate a lack of bottom calculations for forecast entries. I will suggest a review by Scott to apply his template, subject to his availability, in time for the July 2nd deadline.

Hi Briana!


I'm so grateful to Tracy Joseph for connecting us. Right now I'm hosting a 90 Day Business Refinement Cohort on Mondays over Zoom starting at 6:00PM EST.


The purpose of the program is to help legitimize side-hustle operations and prepare small business owners to expand activity beyond the borders of their friends and family circle. Summary/Action: The client and I had a phone call to discuss the SBIR/STTR programs in general: Brief History of the SBIR Program, SBIR Program Objectives, SBIR Eligibility, SBIR Three Phases, Brief History of the STTR Program, STTR Program Objectives, STTR Small Business Eligibility, STTR Non-Profit Institution Eligibility, STTR Three Phases, Advantages of SBIR/STTR Programs, Three Types of SBIR Projects, Contract Agencies, Grant Agencies, Research Topics and Agency Solicitations, Phase I success, Phase II success, Possible SBIR Timeline, Is SBIR/STTR right for the company? We went over the details of the program and how I can work with them. I told them that I am a high-energy physicist who had a small business in Virginia before coming to Michigan. The R&D of our particle accelerator research company was funded by the SBIR program and we had over 30 awards (phase I and II) in 8 of the federal agencies before selling off the business in 2001. Instead of forming another busines
The request is now complete.

Summary/Action: I have taken a look at their commercialization and broader impacts document for their NSF FastTrack proposal, and it is very well written. That said, I do have several comments, suggestions, and recommendations for they. The way I review is to start with general comments about the proposal and then take each section, one by one, in order. Hopefully, this makes it easier for they to follow along with their document.  They should note that they know their technology best. My background is in high-energy physics with a business degree added in. My industry was explosives detection and areas associated with electron accelerators. That gives me a good general technical knowledge, but it doesn’t make me an expert in their field or industry. While I will review as I would if I were their reviewer(s), if something I say or suggest goes against what they think is right, they should certainly ignore it. While I am good at what I do, no one is perfect, and I might not understan

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
I met with our client by zoom call. We discussed some financing options: a second mortgage on their home; business loan; CDFI. The biggest issue for a heavier drone operation is getting the proper certifications and FAA authority. She is told that it could take up to six months until the pilot is approved. Jennifer is seeking her Pilot in Charge authority and her husband is being certified for mixing chemicals for farm application.


We discussed that there are several levels of entry: I suggested buying the smaller drone with a lower cost because it is less to care during the approval process. Once approved she could purchase several drones without the waiting period. What she would like to do is borrow $150K for two J-100 drones, the support trailer with mixing tanks and the back up batteries. This is a large amount to carry while not being able to support the operation with business income during the waiting period.


J-100 Cost= $40.3K each; smaller

AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

The owner is being presented with a partnership opportunity for a Marine City restaurant location, but is hesitant about pursuing a partnership. He is more inclined toward a traditional lease arrangement. At the same time, another potential location in St. Clair is still under consideration and would require a full buildout.


RECOMMENDATIONS AND ACTIONS TO BE TAKEN:

  • Evaluate financial feasibility and control implications of the proposed 50/50 profit-sharing partnership for the Marine City site.
  • Consider negotiating terms that provide more autonomy while still leveraging the other party's investment in the Marine City buildout.
  • Assess the viability and cost of the St. Clair plaza location, including buildout requirements and long-term lease structure.


NEXT STEPS & FOLLOW-UP ACTIONS:

  • Review the projections on

CMURC also assists small b

Maria is facing the challenge of preparing her women's health startup, Fox Health, for investor engagement while simultaneously finalizing her financial forecast and equity tracking systems. With a launch planned for later this summer, she is focused on accurately projecting user growth, revenue, and cap table dynamics in support of a $2 million pre-seed raise. Her ambitious target of 10 million users by 2029 and a 30-40% premium conversion rate requires a solid financial model, and she’s working to organize her revenue projections and advertising assumptions accordingly. She’s also thinking carefully about how to structure and communicate her employee equity pool, track vesting, and align investor expectations with industry benchmarks.


I walked Maria through a forecasting spreadsheet, showing her how to model revenue growth from a base of 10,000 users with 10% monthly growth and 20% premium conversion, while also building in ad-based revenue for non-paying user

Sean is developing Techmate, an AI-powered chat tool designed to provide tech support for non-technical users, but he's facing challenges around user drop-off, limited feedback, and uncertainty about how to prioritize between B2C and B2B opportunities. He’s found that onboarding friction and the requirement for account creation are driving away users before they see the product’s value. Additionally, he's navigating how to gather meaningful customer insights, explore monetization strategies, and determine the right marketing channels—all while working solo and trying to move quickly in a fast-evolving AI landscape.


I recommended removing as many onboarding barriers as possible—allowing anonymous use, adding sign-in with Apple/Google options, and deferring account creation until necessary. We discussed strategies for collecting feedback through customer interviews and small-scale marketing experiments to identify the most effective channels. I emphasized the

*HRS are cumulative (Uplift/TRYE Team)


AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

Conducted thorough research and preparation for our scheduled Zoom call with Victoria Brown on June 17th, focusing on her needs related to supporting at-risk and homeless youth in Flint and Genesee County. During our preparation, we identified key areas of concern, including understanding her ideal customer for a workbook she is developing and navigating existing community systems and relationships that aid this demographic.

Stacy is feeling pressured by the Marketing Company she has been working with to move into the next phase for another $12,000 but she feels like she needs to use and implement the tools and tricks the company taught her before she gives them more money.


Stacy and I are going to work on a plan to build her UP reach for her "local" company. We talked about a big push for Marquette finding those people via Facebook targeted ads.


We are going to meet every Monday and every Wednesday. On the opposing Wednesdays Stacy will meet with Jordan to cover Wave accounting.

Summary: The client sent a copy of their NSF Project Pitch for review.


Action: I have looked over their document and, as it is explained here, I do not think that it will be a fit for NSF. I am just not seeing the actual technology innovation involved. What I am understanding from this is that they are using existing technology (in the form of AI and machine learning) to manipulate existing data into an easier-to-use format. Which has great commercial potential and is definitely a market innovation, but I am not seeing a new base technology that they are creating.  I see that they are trying to counter my argument but stating why other firms couldn’t do what they are proposing, but the simple fact is that, I believe, that if they gave talented people the general idea and enough money and time, they could manipulate AI and ML to get the same sort of outcome. That doesn’t mean they will, but there is no technical feature that they alone own or only have access to tha

Summary/Action: I wrote the client that it was nice to talk to them yesterday. As promised from their meeting: NSF: The NSF Project Pitch template. Remember that this is required as they MUST get an invitation to apply from the NSF before they can submit a full proposal. This can take up to 4 weeks to get a reply but usually takes about 1 week. The NSF topic areas (which they will need to submit their project pitch) are found here. Remember that these areas are fixed but the subtopics are non-exhaustive and they will see at the end of each topic an “other” subtopic. If there is a subtopic that matches their technology, they should use that category, but if not, they would still like to look at it under the other category. The NSF Phase I full proposal template to give they an idea of what they need to prepare for when they get the pitch invitation. It may also help they start thinking through their project plan and refine what they want to put into their pitch. NIH: NIH funding oppo

Tyler had an interview for SBSH grant a few weeks ago. Said there were 130 applicants and they will award 15. There will be two additional rounds later this year. Said they would inform him one way or another within 2-3 weeks so I expect we will know before July 4.

The business is in the early stages of marketing development and brand visibility. The client is initiating efforts but needs structured strategies to build momentum and reach the target audience effectively.


RECOMMENDATIONS AND ACTIONS TO BE TAKEN:

  • Leverage the networking group to promote the brand, gather referrals, and create collaborative opportunities.
  • Implement the Facebook marketing strategy to drive engagement and traffic to YouTube and other platforms.


NEXT STEPS & FOLLOW-UP ACTIONS:

  • Monitor performance analytics on Facebook and YouTube to adjust content and posting strategies.
  • Follow up to assess networking group benefits and connections made.

Alex

A colleague shared this perspective with me today:


Hi Wendy, SMBX is just another crowdfunding/crowdlending platform like GoFundMe, Kickstarter, Kiva, etc. For him to have success on any crowdfunding platform, it's really more of a marketing ploy--creating a compelling vision that people want to get behind. From a cash flow perspective, he should build in the bond principal and interest payments into his plan to see if he can make the monthly bond payments if he actually received funding. You can use an amortizing loan calculator to estimate the monthly cost.

Nicole reached out to me after having not heard from her in a couple of months. She seemed a bit confused on what to do after I reviewed her business plan and said it looked good. I told her that she could work with any local financers such as her credit union to get a commercial loan. She seemed disappointed as she was under the impression that the SBDC would "submit" her business plan to lenders. I explained that we work on the consulting side and help with preparing clients to speak to lenders, but that we do not actually file anything or get loans on the client's behalf. Nicole let me know that she would be going elsewhere to seek assistance and I asked that she please reach out if she needs anything else. I will close her case for now as it seems unlikely from our conversation that she will be reaching out again.

Call with David. The price is $30k for the food truck. The owner, Dan, said David can apply 50% of retained earnings (money in the bank account) towards the purchase price on October 1. So, the idea is that the food truck as $20k in the bank on 10/1. That would reduce the purchase price down to $20k. I think the money in the bank would go to the owner and not stay wihtt he business, but needs clarification. It seems like the sale price could be $20k. I asked for an equipment list an d3 years of P&L's. Perhaps we can get a scratch valuation from Dan Brophy.


David said the truck currently pays $1000 in rent to Silver Spruce (its $3k at Little Fleet per month). David asked if he could cut the rent in half. Or they pay the $1000/mo, but escrow $500 mo to be used towards the purchase price. They are working on some kind of partnership deal to keep the Bull and Bear there. David said business is down at the food truck and Silver Spruce. I wonder if will still mak

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Client: Mike (First name only for confidentiality)

Background: Mike is a highly dedicated veteran with three tours in Iraq, where he worked extensively with other veterans. He has a strong educational background with a degree in Criminal Justice and a Master's in Social Work. His wife's family has a history of successful family businesses, providing some familial exposure to entrepreneurship. Mike's deep commitment to supporting veterans stems from his experience with the Bay Area Vet non-profit and the observed spike in veteran suicides post-COVID. He is currently pursuing a specialized certification from MSU to work specifically with combat veterans.

Business Concept: Mike's core business idea is to establish a recreational therapy resort for veterans and their spouses. This unique approach aims to provide a non-traditional, less sterile environment for counseling and therapy, recognizing that many vete

Client can still weigh what he wants to offer once we get the projections in place. Deadline is no longer July 1st as that's unrealistic at this point. Looking more at an August 1st target now. (won't break down into m-t-m) Client will send us the data and we'll input it into a 2022 - 2027 set of data for client's analysis and ability to take to lender/make counter offer to owner. 4,000 Donuts - Teri is questioning if she should buy the business with a monthly loan payment of $3,919.50.


We crunched the tax return numbers for both 2023 and 2018 (for a pre-pandemic picture) to see how much the business could make. $50,537 estimated expenses

$47,035 estimated loan payment

$25,000 Teri take home

$ COGS are missing

$122,572 Total of above divided by 12 months is $10,214

Summary: The client asked me to review the Phase 1 R&D Plan and suggest ways to shorten the document. 


Action: I responded that I have played with the document a bit and I do have some suggestions, both for the document contents and for shortening. To start with, some simple things to save space. First, they are not single-spaced all the way through. Highlight the whole thing and make sure they are. Second, because they are in 10pt Arial (which is great), but they have an “after space” of 12pt, they are wasting a little bit of space there. So change the after space to 10pt or eliminate the after space and just to a paragraph return. Third, take out the page numbers. They aren’t allowed to have them in the final copy, and sometimes the footer complicates the bottom margin, especially in uploads. The system will put the page numbers in for they automatically. My fourth general suggestion is to have someone go through this with a fine-tooth comb for brevity. I am v

Plans fell through on the building acquisition along with the commitment from the other partners. On hold for this business and will close for now.

1.I've been focused on concert recordings for the past few years but I've been having such a hard time breaking into that market that I'm currently in the process of changing my niche. My new target niche is local trades businesses — contractors, plumbers, electricians, remodelers, mechanics, etc.

Pitch Competition Question



Ashley Kirby

EIN Assistance


  • Rebecca, a TIN or Taxpayer Identification Number is issued by the state, and the EIN is issued by the IRS. Here are the steps:
  • Apply for a New EIN (for the LLC)
    • Go to the IRS application: https://www.irs.gov/businesses/small-businesses-self-employed/apply-for-an-employer-identification-number-ein-online
    • Select that you are starting a new business and choose “Limited Liability Company (LLC)”
    • Indicate the number of members and the state (Michigan)
    • You will be asked to provide a responsible party’s SSN (this is required even for LLCs—it doesn't mean the EIN is tied to a person like a sole prop)
    • Submit and download the EIN confirmation letter immediately
  • Notify Agencies of New EIN
    • Update Michigan Treasury</

Osha and I met for her 2nd meeting. We discussed the pro forma which was confusing her and I explained which parts were relevant to her and which were unnecessary for a simple service business. She may ask me to review her next effort. We also discussed her marketing and outreach efforts, including her website, which she will get redone through the milestone grant. All of her referrals have come through word of mouth, and she doesn't necessarily want to get more exposure to a general audience. (She did a local radio show and it got her thinking).


She has hire a biller and seems set on trying to do 2 births / month during this "Phase I" of her business. She believes that that will give her optimum work-life balance. She's learning much about the realities of her practice (insurance, Medicaid, etc) and knows it will evolve over time.

Zoom with Nikunj following email exchange on June 12. He has made a lot of progress since the last substantive conversation in early 2024. The product is now a trailer with deployable solar arrays and a 5kw battery. He received $25k investment from ID Ventures and subsequent funding from AA Spark to develop and build prototype units in India and the US. They were used for product and market testing. The India program led to the sale of 2 units. The US built unit was used to power the Container Globe theatre for the spring season. Product development direction is toward swappable battery and control modules on the trailer for different use cases. He envisions increasing battery size to 20kw, then 50 and 100 kw. The AAS funded prototype was built by Beacon at NewLab. It has higher power capacity than the initial Indian unit. His market focus is rural areas, emergency situations, agriculture. This differs from Electric Outdoors, which is aimed at luxury glamping applications. He

Suhan is in the early stages of building a 3D virtualization platform and facing the common startup challenge of having too many potential use cases without clear customer validation. He’s excited about applications ranging from real estate to manufacturing simulations, but hasn’t yet identified a specific segment with a strong enough pain point to prioritize. Without that focus, it’s difficult to make confident product decisions, shape a go-to-market strategy, or begin building meaningful traction—all of which are essential before approaching investors or pursuing further development.


I recommended Suhan pause product development until he has clarity on his beachhead customer. I shared guidance on how to structure a customer discovery process and emphasized that selecting a specific, well-defined segment is foundational for long-term success. We discussed why short sales cycles, urgent customer pain points, and easy access to early adopters make certain segments—li

Talk to you soon,


Charlie

Linda’s company, a bootstrapped SAAS technology for home care agencies, is facing several important transitions. She’s preparing to launch a new lower-cost product, “Rosemark Light,” while also managing a long-term software modernization effort and shifting from a Windows-based system to a more accessible web-based platform. Though her company serves about 300 clients and has grown steadily, she sees opportunity in simplifying the sales process, improving client onboarding, and tapping into a larger share of the market. Linda expressed concern about the friction caused by their $250 onboarding fee and shared that past efforts at offering free trials didn’t result in meaningful engagement or respect for the product.


I recommended running an experiment to remove the onboarding fee for one to two months and monitor conversion results. We also discussed the potential of a “no-touch” onboarding model using feature-limited free tiers—such as offering up to 500 scheduled a
The request is now complete.

Summary: The client responded to my review of their NSF Pitch. They can definitely address some of the points they highlighted to make their pitch more robust and more generalizable to a broader community including the DoD. Here are some suggestions from Matt on how to bolster the technology side. While these may not all be done in the first pass they could be coming. They wanted to know if these meet the criteria they mentioned and if certain points stand out more than others please highlight those for them to emphasize.  1. A Turnkey, White-Label Platform for Employers & Payers Pre-built integrations with major EHRs (Epic, Cerner, Athena) so health systems can drop it in as a branded “Care Companion.” Offer a self-service portal where HR teams or payers spin up cohorts, upload their population data, and instantly get tailored reporting and engagement flows. Revenue model: per-employee-per-month (PEPM) or outcome-based pricing (e.g. shared savings if complicated-care admissions

Summary: The client wrote that they were able to get their SAM and CAGE registration done last week. They sent over the latest draft of one of their proposals for an Airforce topic for a quick review.


Action: I responded that there is a lot here that does not meet the requirements of the Air Force document. I started writing it up and decided it would probably be easier on both of them if I just created the template to help they along. I have attached it here. It should help they determine the sections, how they should go, which ones are part of the 20-page limit (only volume 2 with its 12 sections), and how to do the other volumes. As a side note for the 20 pages, they should be at least 18 pages if not the full 20. It is very rare that someone comes in at much less than the limit and the reviewers don’t look kindly on that. 20 pages is not a lot for this type of application and it concerns them if they aren’t taking all the space they are offering. It is importan

Summary/Action: Jim, Melanie and I had a conversation about the NIH SBIR program. I told them that I am a high-energy physicist who had a small business in Virginia before coming to Michigan. The R&D of our particle accelerator research company was funded by the SBIR program and we had over 30 awards (phase I and II) in 8 of the federal agencies before selling off the business in 2001. Instead of forming another business, I came to the SBDC to help others utilize the SBIR programs. We then honed in on NIH and the SBIR that they submitted and got rejected. I had looked over their review comments and I think a first step is to reach out to the program manager and discuss how fits for them and suggestions that they have. I also recommended looking at specific SBIR opportunities in NIH. I told them that I would send more information and templates:  NIH funding opportunities (for both the general areas and specific requests that they talked about) can be found here. The HHS programs
review demo reports and provide to client for review.
